summary: I currently live at Pangborn Hall, which is part of the community of priests and brothers of Holy Cross at Notre Dame. It’s where I reside as part of my religious life and ministry. Living there connects me closely with the Holy Cross community on campus.

Holy Cross is the religious community I belong to, consisting of priests and brothers at Notre Dame. Much of our life is communal, including common prayer and meals, which was deeply affected by the pandemic. Being part of Holy Cross means living and working closely with others in faith and service.

I serve and live at the University of Notre Dame, where I was rector of the Basilica of the Sacred Heart and lived at Old College Undergraduate Seminary before the pandemic. Notre Dame’s campus life, including football seasons and student formation, was vibrant until COVID-19 changed everything in 2020. It’s been a place of both ministry and community for me.

Before the pandemic, I was the rector of the Basilica of the Sacred Heart at Notre Dame. My role involved overseeing the liturgical experience, scheduling, and maintenance of the Basilica. It was my first year as rector in 2020, and I was learning the full scope of responsibilities when COVID-19 forced us to suspend in-person masses and move to livestreaming.

We first heard about COVID-19 in January 2020, initially thinking it might be like previous outbreaks. I was in London at the time, and the world quickly shut down, forcing a rapid return to the U.S. The pandemic drastically changed our communal religious life at Notre Dame, suspending in-person masses and shifting to online worship, which was a huge adjustment for all of us.

Having lived through the H1N1 influenza outbreak before, I initially thought COVID-19 might be similar in impact. H1N1 was one of the significant disease outbreaks that made news but didn’t affect our daily lives as deeply. This past experience shaped my early perspective on the unfolding pandemic in 2020.

entity: Ebola virus epidemic in West Africa
summary: The Ebola virus epidemic in West Africa was another major health crisis I witnessed from afar. Like H1N1, it was a serious outbreak but didn’t directly impact our community here in the U.S. This history of global epidemics helped me initially gauge the seriousness of COVID-19 before it hit home.

I was studying at the seminary in the Archdiocese of Westminster in London when the pandemic began. It was spring break at Notre Dame, and I traveled there on a business trip. The Archdiocese was part of the sudden lockdown and recall of study abroad students, forcing a quick departure back to the U.S.

I was in London on a business trip in early 2020 when the COVID-19 pandemic caused the world to shut down. Being there during the spring break of Notre Dame, I had to quickly leave as study abroad students were recalled. The urgency to exit London within 24 hours was intense, marking a pivotal moment in the pandemic’s early days.

During the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, I was in the United Kingdom, specifically London, when the government announced lockdowns and travel restrictions. There was real concern about being stuck there, so I had to leave quickly. This experience underscored how rapidly the pandemic was affecting international travel and daily life.

Easter 2020 was unlike any other because it happened during the early weeks of the COVID-19 pandemic. At Notre Dame’s Basilica of the Sacred Heart, we suspended in-person masses and held services with only a small group of Holy Cross religious present, spaced six feet apart. We livestreamed the liturgies, which was a major shift from our usual communal celebrations.

South Bend, Indiana is where Notre Dame is located, and it’s where I live and serve as part of the Holy Cross community. The pandemic deeply affected life here, especially on campus, where communal activities had to be restructured for safety. Despite the challenges, South Bend remained our home base throughout 2020.

Zoom became an essential tool for us during the pandemic to maintain communication and community. We used it for meetings, prayer groups, and staying connected with seminarians who were at home. Having prior experience with Zoom and Skype made the transition easier, but it still couldn’t fully replace in-person pastoral care.

Before the pandemic, we had already used Skype for academic and community communication, so it was somewhat familiar when COVID-19 forced us to rely more on video calls. Skype, along with Zoom, helped us keep in touch with students and colleagues remotely. It was part of the technology that eased the transition to virtual interaction in 2020.
